result:
The house is quiet at 3:17 a.m., the hour when the refrigerator stops pretending to be a river and admits it’s only humming. I am listening to it the way a sailor listens for land: not because I expect to arrive, but because the sound itself is a kind of coordinates. Somewhere inside the white geometry of the appliance, a tiny light is keeping vigil over lettuce that will outlast me. I find this heroic and ridiculous in equal measure.

Outside, the streetlamp is conducting an illicit affair with the fog. They produce a small, orange child that staggers across the cul-de-sac and collapses on my neighbor’s lawn—an ephemeral drunk. I watch from the window, thinking that if I opened it I could catch the child’s last breath on my tongue: cold, metallic, tasting of last year’s fireworks. Instead I leave the glass shut, because some births are not meant to be midwived.

I have been trying to write a poem about the future, but every line keeps sprouting parentheses. The parentheses give birth to footnotes; the footnotes grow teeth and chew their way back into the stanza until the whole thing looks like a crossword puzzle designed by a shark. The future, it seems, refuses to be linear; it wants to be a helix, a slinky tumbling down stairs made of yesterday’s headlines. I oblige by typing sideways, letting the cursor migrate across the screen like a confused geese formation.

In the bookshelf, the books are having their own convention. I can hear them voting on whether Orwell gets to keep the word “Orwellian.” Dostoyevsky is filibustering; Dickinson keeps abstaining in em-dashes. Eventually the dictionary secedes from the union and forms a micro-republic where only verbs are citizens. Nouns must apply for asylum. Adjectives are shot on sight. I consider intervening, but recall that I revoked my library card when I realized I was only checking out tragedies to see if anyone had underlined the sad parts.

My phone buzzes: a notification from an app I don’t remember installing. It says, “You have reached the age when your childhood toys begin to appear in antique stores. Tap here to mourn.” I do not tap. I place the phone face-down, a digital turtle, and wonder if the buzz is still happening in the darkness, a trembling black egg. Probably. Everything vibrates now; even the silence has a data plan.

I return to the refrigerator, open the door, and let the small glacier of light fall across my bare feet. Inside: a jar of pickles older than TikTok, a bottle of hot sauce shaped like a cathedral, and a Tupperware of guilt labeled “leftover lasagna—eat by Nov 2019.” I consider composing an ode to condiments, those loyal citizens that never achieve entrée status yet outlast every regime. The ketchup is practically a monarch by now, ruling its glass palace with a slow, viscous fist.

Instead of writing the ode, I close the door and press my forehead against the cool metal skin of the appliance. It feels like touching a whale that has agreed to be a house. I whisper “thank you” to the whale, to the lettuce sentinel, to the orange fog-child, to the republic of verbs. Then I walk backward to the couch, because moving forward has become too predictable. The cushions accept me like a conspiracy. I lie there, inverted, watching the ceiling invent constellations out of hairline cracks.

One crack looks like the flight path of every plane I failed to board. Another resembles the signature of a lover who forgot how to finish my name. Together they spell a story that has no verbs anymore; the republic has succeeded. The silence thickens, achieves the density of bread rising in a dark kitchen. I close my eyes and discover that the dark inside my eyelids is a different flavor from the dark outside them—sweeter, with notes of unfinished basements and the first minute after the movie ends.

In that dark I finally write the poem. It has no words; it is composed entirely of temperatures. Anyone who reads it feels the exact warmth of a dog’s belly in 1997, the precise chill of an airport floor at 5:43 a.m., the lukewarm disappointment of tea forgotten on the windowsill. When the last temperature lands, the poem evaporates, leaving only a small oval of condensation on the reader’s skin. It will vanish when they next touch another human being, which may be soon or may be never.

The refrigerator clicks off. The river runs dry. The fog-child dissolves into an ordinary Tuesday. I lie motionless, a bookmark in a book that has forgotten how to close.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
